passages.explanation: Agrippa tells Paul he may speak for himself. Paul stretches out his hand and begins his defence — his most polished and literary speech in Acts. He considers himself fortunate to make his case before Agrippa, who is well acquainted with all Jewish customs and controversies. Paul asks for patience. All the Jews know how he lived from the beginning — in his own nation and in Jerusalem. They've known him for a long time and can testify that he lived as a Pharisee, the strictest sect of their religion. Now he stands on trial because of his hope in what God promised the ancestors — the very promise the twelve tribes hope to see fulfilled as they earnestly serve God day and night. King Agrippa, it's because of this hope that the Jews are accusing me. Why should any of you consider it incredible that God raises the dead? Paul frames the entire case around resurrection — not as a fringe belief, but as the fulfilment of Israel's deepest hope.

Paul recounts his former life: I was convinced I ought to do everything possible to oppose the name of Jesus of Nazareth. In Jerusalem he put many believers in prison with the chief priests' authority. When they were put to death, he cast his vote against them. He punished them in synagogue after synagogue, tried to force them to blaspheme, and in raging fury pursued them even to foreign cities. Paul doesn't minimise his past — he paints himself as the most unlikely candidate for conversion, which makes the Damascus road all the more compelling.

Paul tells the Damascus road story a third time in Acts, this time with additional details suited to a royal audience. On the road at midday, he saw a light from heaven brighter than the sun, blazing around him and his companions. They all fell to the ground. A voice spoke in Aramaic: Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It's hard for you to kick against the goads — a Greek proverb about resisting the divine will. Jesus identifies himself and commissions Paul directly: I'm appointing you as a servant and witness. I'm sending you to the Gentiles to open their eyes and turn them from darkness to light, from the power of Satan to God, so they may receive forgiveness of sins and a place among those who are sanctified by faith in me. This version compresses Ananias' role and presents the commission as coming directly from Jesus — appropriate for an audience with a king.

Paul declares: I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision. First in Damascus, then Jerusalem, then throughout Judea, and then to the Gentiles, he preached that they should repent, turn to God, and demonstrate their repentance by their deeds. This is why the Jews seized him in the temple and tried to kill him. But God has helped him to this day, and he stands testifying to small and great alike, saying nothing beyond what the prophets and Moses said would happen: that the Messiah would suffer, be the first to rise from the dead, and proclaim light to both the Jewish people and the Gentiles. Paul's argument is that he's not teaching anything new — he's teaching what Moses and the prophets always said.

Festus interrupts: you're out of your mind, Paul! Your great learning is driving you insane! Paul responds calmly: I'm not insane, most excellent Festus — I'm speaking true and rational words. The king knows about these things, and I can speak freely to him. None of this happened in a corner. Then Paul turns directly to Agrippa: King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know you do. Agrippa's reply is famous but ambiguous: do you think that in such a short time you can persuade me to be a Christian? Paul answers: short time or long — I pray to God that not only you but everyone listening today may become what I am, except for these chains. Paul holds up his shackles. It's a devastating rhetorical moment: the prisoner offers the king something the king can't give himself.

The king, the governor, Bernice, and everyone with them get up and leave. As they withdraw, they talk among themselves: this man is doing nothing that deserves death or imprisonment. Agrippa tells Festus: this man could have been set free if he hadn't appealed to Caesar. Paul's innocence is formally acknowledged — by a governor and a king. But the appeal stands, and Paul must go to Rome. What looks like a legal miscalculation is actually the fulfilment of God's plan.
